enrichment.short_description: Kavita is a free, open-source, self-hosted digital library server for reading and organizing manga, comics, webtoons and ebooks.
enrichment.seo_title: Kavita Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kavita is a free, self-hosted digital library for manga, comics and ebooks. See features, setup requirements, pricing and top alternatives like Komga.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Kavita is a free and open-source, self-hosted reading server created by the Kareadita organization and first released publicly in 2020; the project is licensed under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPLv3) and is maintained on GitHub, where by mid-2026 it has attracted over 11,000 stars, more than 600 forks, and dozens of active contributors, reflecting its status as one of the most widely adopted community-run alternatives to commercial digital library and comic reading platforms. Rather than being run by a venture-funded company, Kavita is built and sustained by volunteer developers who ship frequent releases (the project counts more than 75 tagged releases) and is currently labeled beta software under continuous active development. Architecturally, Kavita is a lightweight server application, written primarily in C# (.NET) on the backend with a TypeScript/Angular frontend, that a user installs on their own hardware, home server, NAS, or cloud VPS via Docker, a native binary, or platform-specific packages; once running, it scans watched folders for media files and serves a responsive web interface plus an OPDS feed to any device with a browser or OPDS-compatible reading app, meaning users retain full ownership and control of their files rather than uploading them to a third-party cloud service. The platform supports an unusually broad range of formats in a single application, including archive-based comics and manga (CBZ, CBR, CBT, CB7, ZIP, RAR, RAR5, 7ZIP, TAR.GZ, and raw image folders) as well as ebook formats (EPUB2, EPUB3, and PDF), and it ships purpose-built readers for each content type: a manga/webtoon reader with single-page, double-page, and continuous vertical webtoon modes, and a dedicated EPUB reader with customizable typography, themes, and reading settings. Kavita's feature set extends well beyond basic file serving. It offers automatic library scanning and metadata matching, rich metadata editing, custom collections and reading lists that can be curated and shared between users on the same server, bookmarking and highlighting with note-taking that can export to Obsidian, full-text and metadata-driven search across an entire library, and granular multi-user access control that lets an administrator restrict specific libraries or apply age-based content restrictions per account, with each user maintaining independent reading progress, want-to-read lists, and bookmarks. Authentication can be handled through Kavita's built-in accounts or through OpenID Connect (OIDC) for organizations wanting single sign-on. Additional conveniences include one-click send-to-Kindle/e-reader functionality, OPDS support so the library can be browsed from third-party apps, and a REST API that enables community-built companion apps and integrations. Because Kavita is fully open source and self-hosted, there is no subscription fee, no per-user pricing tier, and no vendor lock-in; the only ongoing cost is whatever hardware or hosting the user already runs (commonly a home server, Synology/QNAP NAS, Raspberry Pi, or a small cloud instance), making the effective pricing model free forever. This puts Kavita in the self-hosted media server category alongside tools like Plex, Jellyfin, and Calibre-Web for books, but its closest direct competitors are other purpose-built comic/manga library servers such as Komga (also free and open source, Java-based) and the discontinued Ubooquity, as well as commercial cloud reading services; Kavita differentiates itself through its combination of native manga/webtoon-optimized readers, deep metadata and collection tooling, multi-user permission granularity, and an actively growing plugin and companion-app ecosystem. The target audience is technically comfortable hobbyists, homelab enthusiasts, and small groups (families, friend circles, book/manga clubs) who own digital comic, manga, and ebook collections and want a private, ad-free, centrally organized way to read them from any device without relying on a commercial storefront or subscription service. Because it requires self-hosting knowledge (Docker, reverse proxy configuration, and basic server administration), Kavita is less suited to non-technical users seeking a zero-setup consumer product, but for its target self-hosting community it has become one of the most recommended tools in the space, frequently discussed on homelab and self-hosting forums as the go-to solution for a private manga and comic library.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kavita is a free, open-source reading server built by the Kareadita community and first released in 2020 under the GPLv3 license. It lets anyone turn their own hardware, whether a home server, NAS, or cloud VPS, into a private digital library for manga, comics, webtoons and ebooks, accessible from any browser or OPDS-compatible app.

Unlike commercial cloud reading subscriptions, Kavita keeps every file under the user's own control. It scans watched folders, pulls in metadata, and serves the content through dedicated readers, with no recurring fees, ads, or vendor lock-in at any point.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Kavita supports an unusually wide range of formats in one application, from archive-based comics (CBZ, CBR, CB7, ZIP, RAR, 7ZIP) to ebooks (EPUB2, EPUB3, PDF), each served through a purpose-built reader with single-page, double-page, and continuous webtoon display modes.

Beyond reading, Kavita offers granular multi-user permissions with per-library and age-based restrictions, custom reading lists and collections, highlighting and note export to Obsidian, OIDC single sign-on, and a REST API and OPDS feed for third-party app compatibility.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Kavita has no pricing tiers, subscriptions, or paywalled features of any kind. The entire application is free and open source, funded purely through volunteer development rather than commercial revenue.

The only real cost is infrastructure: users need their own server or hosting environment capable of running Docker or a native binary, which for most homelab users means an existing NAS, Raspberry Pi, or small VPS they already own.

features.title: Multi-format library
features.description: Supports CBZ, CBR, CB7, ZIP, RAR, 7ZIP, PDF and EPUB in a single unified server.

features.title: Purpose-built readers
features.description: Dedicated manga, webtoon and EPUB readers with single-page, double-page and continuous scroll modes.

features.title: Multi-user access control
features.description: Per-library and age-based restrictions with independent reading progress for each account.

features.title: Metadata and collections
features.description: Automatic library scanning, metadata editing, and curated reading lists shareable between users.

features.title: OPDS and REST API
features.description: Exposes the library to third-party e-reader apps and enables custom integrations.

features.title: Send to device
features.description: One-click delivery of content to Kindle and other e-readers for offline reading.

features.title: OIDC authentication
features.description: Supports single sign-on for organizations alongside built-in login accounts.

features.title: Highlighting and notes
features.description: In-book highlighting and note-taking with export support to Obsidian.

pros: Completely free and open source with no premium tier
Broad format support in a single application, covering comics, manga, webtoons and ebooks
Strong multi-user permission and parental-control system
Active development with frequent releases and a large community
Full data ownership since everything runs on the user's own server
cons: Requires self-hosting knowledge, including Docker and reverse proxy setup
Still labeled beta software with occasional bugs
No official vendor support or SLA
No built-in content acquisition, users must supply their own files
Mobile experience relies on the web app or third-party clients rather than a first-party app

faqs.question: Is Kavita free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Kavita is completely free and open source under the GPLv3 license, with no premium tiers or paid add-ons.

faqs.question: What file formats does Kavita support?
faqs.answer: Kavita supports CBZ, CBR, CBT, CB7, ZIP, RAR, RAR5, 7ZIP, TAR.GZ, raw image folders, EPUB2, EPUB3 and PDF.

faqs.question: Does Kavita require self-hosting?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kavita must be installed on your own server, NAS, or VPS via Docker, a native binary, or a platform package.

faqs.question: Can multiple people use one Kavita server?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kavita supports multiple user accounts with granular per-library and age-based permission controls.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Kavita and Komga?
faqs.answer: Both are free self-hosted comic servers; Kavita additionally offers native ebook readers for EPUB and PDF plus OIDC login support.

faqs.question: Does Kavita have mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Kavita is accessed through a responsive web app and OPDS-compatible reading apps; there is no single official first-party mobile app.

use_cases.title: Personal manga and comic library
use_cases.description: Collectors organize and read their manga, comic and webtoon collections from a private, self-hosted server.

use_cases.title: Family ebook and comic sharing
use_cases.description: Households share a common library while keeping separate reading progress and age-based access per family member.

use_cases.title: Homelab media stack addition
use_cases.description: Self-hosting enthusiasts add Kavita alongside tools like Plex or Jellyfin to complete a private media server setup.

features: OPDS feed and REST API access
Community support via Discord and GitHub

enrichment.short_description: Kayako is a customer service and help desk platform offering ticketing, live chat, a shared inbox and pay-per-resolution AI ticket automation.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing (Kay AI Agent from $1 per resolved ticket)
enrichment.seo_title: Kayako Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kayako help desk software review covering ticketing, SingleView, live chat, knowledge base and Kay AI pricing at 1 dollar per resolved ticket, plus alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Kayako is a customer service and help desk software platform founded in 2001, with origins in Jalandhar, India, before the company established its headquarters in London, United Kingdom; it also maintains additional offices in Gurgaon, India and Singapore. In February 2018 Kayako was acquired by ESW Capital, a private equity firm based in Austin, Texas that specializes in acquiring and modernizing enterprise software businesses, and Kayako has continued to operate and evolve its product under that ownership. Over more than two decades the company has built a customer base it describes as roughly 50,000 organizations across over 100 countries, with named customers including Peugeot, De Beers, NASA, and the American Motorcyclist Association. Kayako's core product is a help desk and customer support platform built around a shared team inbox and ticketing system: incoming customer messages from email, live chat, and self-service channels are consolidated into a single queue that support agents work from, with the platform's signature SingleView feature giving agents a unified view of a customer's full conversation history, past tickets, and profile data across every channel in one screen rather than forcing agents to piece together context from separate tools. Around that ticketing core, Kayako provides a self-service knowledge base and customer portal, a live chat widget for websites, SLA management and automated ticket routing rules, autoresponders, custom fields and workflow automation, reporting and analytics dashboards, and an API for building integrations with third-party systems. The most significant recent change to the product, reflected on Kayako's current pricing page, is a pivot toward AI-first, resolution-based pricing: rather than the traditional per-agent-seat subscription model used by most help desk vendors, Kayako introduced Kay, an AI support agent priced at $1 per fully resolved ticket, with no seat fees, no setup fees, and no charge for escalations or partial resolutions; the company states Kay achieves roughly a 68 percent autonomous resolution rate and positions this per-resolution cost of around $1 as dramatically cheaper than the blended cost of a human-handled ticket, which it estimates at roughly $4.60. Kay can be deployed either as an add-on layered onto a company's existing help desk platform or as part of Kayako One, Kayako's full bundled platform that combines the unified inbox, ticketing, knowledge base, live chat widget, automation rules, reporting, and the Kay AI agent together with white-glove onboarding; pricing for Kayako One itself is not publicly listed and requires contacting Kayako's sales team for a custom quote, a common practice among help desk vendors targeting mid-market and enterprise buyers. Kayako's target audience is small and mid-sized business support teams, as well as mid-market companies, that want an all-in-one help desk without assembling a stack of separate point tools, and that are increasingly interested in offloading routine ticket volume to AI to control support headcount costs. In the competitive help desk landscape, Kayako sits alongside much larger and more heavily funded platforms: Zendesk, which offers over 1,300 marketplace integrations and is generally positioned for large or complex support operations; Freshdesk, which is often cited as faster and cheaper to onboard for small and mid-sized teams with strong AI-assisted ticketing and a polished mobile experience; and Help Scout, which emphasizes simplicity and an email-centric support experience for around 12,000 customers. Reviewers and comparison sites note that Kayako's relative weaknesses versus these larger competitors include a smaller integration marketplace, less advanced native reporting and analytics, and reliance on add-ons for deeper AI functionality outside the core Kay agent. Kayako's differentiation instead rests on its unified SingleView customer context, its long operating history and enterprise customer references, and its newer resolution-based AI pricing model, which removes the seat-based cost scaling that makes competitors more expensive as a support team grows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kayako is a help desk and customer service platform founded in 2001 and now headquartered in London, with additional offices in India and Singapore. Since being acquired by ESW Capital in 2018, the company has continued serving an estimated 50,000 customers across more than 100 countries, including names like NASA and Peugeot.

The platform centers on a shared inbox and ticketing system enhanced by SingleView, a feature that consolidates a customer's full history across email, chat and self-service into one agent screen, reducing the need to jump between tools to understand context.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include ticket management, live chat, a self-service knowledge base, SLA management, automated routing, custom workflows and reporting dashboards, all accessible through an API for third-party integrations.

The newest addition is Kay, an AI support agent priced per resolved ticket rather than per seat, which the company reports resolves roughly 68 percent of tickets autonomously and can be added to an existing help desk or bundled into the full Kayako One platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Kayako has moved away from traditional seat-based pricing toward a resolution-based model for its AI agent: Kay costs $1 per fully resolved ticket with no setup or seat fees, and no charge for escalated or partially resolved conversations.

The full Kayako One platform, bundling ticketing, chat, knowledge base and Kay together with onboarding support, is not listed at a fixed public price and requires contacting Kayako's sales team for a custom quote based on team size and needs.

features.title: Reporting and analytics
features.description: Dashboards for tracking ticket volume, resolution rates and team performance.

features.title: API and integrations
features.description: REST API for connecting Kayako to third-party business systems.

pros: Unified SingleView gives agents full customer context in one screen
Resolution-based Kay AI pricing avoids seat-cost scaling as ticket volume grows
Long operating history since 2001 with enterprise references like NASA and Peugeot
Combines ticketing, chat and knowledge base in one platform
No setup or seat fees for the Kay AI add-on
cons: Smaller integration marketplace than Zendesk
Advanced reporting and analytics are more limited than larger competitors
Kayako One platform pricing is not publicly listed and requires a sales call
Deeper AI functionality outside Kay may require add-ons
Smaller market share means fewer independent implementation partners

faqs.question: What is Kayako?
faqs.answer: Kayako is a customer service and help desk platform offering ticketing, live chat, a knowledge base and AI-driven ticket resolution.

faqs.question: How much does Kayako cost?
faqs.answer: The Kay AI agent costs $1 per resolved ticket with no seat fees; the full Kayako One platform requires a custom quote from sales.

faqs.question: Who owns Kayako?
faqs.answer: Kayako has been owned by ESW Capital, a private equity firm based in Austin, Texas, since February 2018.

faqs.question: What is SingleView?
faqs.answer: SingleView is Kayako's feature that unifies a customer's conversation history and profile data across channels in one agent screen.

faqs.question: Does Kayako offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Kayako's pricing page directs prospective customers toward a demo or sales quote rather than a widely advertised self-serve free trial.

faqs.question: Who are Kayako's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Kayako's primary competitors include Zendesk, Freshdesk and Help Scout.

use_cases.description: Support operations teams add the Kay AI agent to cut per-ticket resolution costs without adding headcount.

features: No charge for escalations or partial resolutions

enrichment.short_description: Kdenlive is a free, open-source, cross-platform non-linear video editor built on the MLT Framework and KDE technologies.
enrichment.seo_title: Kdenlive Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kdenlive review: a free open-source video editor with multi-track timelines, color scopes and speech-to-text. See features, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Kdenlive, an acronym for KDE Non-Linear Video Editor, is a free and open-source, cross-platform video editing application distributed under the GNU General Public License. The project traces its origin to 2002, when developer Jason Wood started it and released an early version 0.2.3 in October 2003; development then stalled for roughly two years until Jean-Baptiste Mardelle took over the project in 2005 and led an extensive rewrite to migrate the codebase from the aging KDE 3 platform to KDE 4 and integrate it with the MLT multimedia framework, a process that concluded around 2008 and was followed by a steady cadence of new releases. Kdenlive became an official KDE project in 2015, and in April 2019 the team shipped a heavily refactored version as part of the KDE Applications 19.04 release, modernizing the timeline engine and stabilizing performance. Today Kdenlive is developed by a community of volunteer contributors under the umbrella of KDE e.V., the nonprofit organization registered in Berlin, Germany that supports the broader KDE free-software community and accepts donations to fund development infrastructure, sprints, and contributor travel; there is no company behind Kdenlive and no commercial licensing tier. Architecturally, Kdenlive's graphical interface is written in C++ using the Qt toolkit and KDE Frameworks libraries, while all of the actual video and audio processing, timeline playback, and rendering is handled underneath by the MLT Framework, an open-source multimedia authoring engine originally created by Dan Dennedy; MLT in turn relies on FFmpeg for decoding and encoding virtually every common video and audio format, including QuickTime, AVI, WMV, MPEG-family formats, and Flash Video, and hosts third-party effects libraries such as Frei0r and Movit for video effects and LADSPA and SoX for audio processing. This layered architecture lets Kdenlive support an unlimited number of video and audio tracks on its multi-track timeline, along with a built-in title and text-clip editor, proxy-clip workflows for editing high-resolution footage smoothly, keyframeable effects and transitions, color scopes such as waveform, histogram and vectorscope for grading, speech-to-text transcription, automatic scene-cut detection, motion tracking, a project bin for organizing source clips, and an extensive library of render presets and export profiles covering standard, HD, and widescreen formats for PAL, NTSC, HDV, and AVCHD standards. Because Kdenlive is entirely free and open source, its pricing model is simply free forever, with the project funded through voluntary donations to KDE e.V. rather than subscriptions, feature-gated tiers, or watermarked exports; it is distributed for Windows, macOS, and Linux, with Linux users typically installing it via their distribution's package manager, Flatpak, or Snap. This makes Kdenlive especially popular in the Linux desktop community, where it is frequently the default recommendation for non-linear video editing, but the Windows and macOS builds have also grown steadily more capable and widely used. Kdenlive's target audience spans hobbyist video creators, students, YouTubers and independent content creators, documentary and short-film editors, and organizations that want a capable, no-cost editor without the recurring subscription cost of commercial software. In the broader video editing market, Kdenlive is most often compared to other free and open-source editors such as Shotcut, which is also FFmpeg and MLT based, and OpenShot, as well as free-tier commercial tools like DaVinci Resolve, and, at the high end, subscription-based professional suites such as Adobe Premiere Pro and Apple's Final Cut Pro. Reviewers generally position Kdenlive as offering a feature set closer to prosumer commercial editors than most free alternatives, particularly in areas like keyframeable effects, proxy editing, and multi-track flexibility, while DaVinci Resolve is typically favored for advanced color grading and Fairlight audio post-production, and Shotcut and OpenShot are seen as simpler, lighter-weight options for basic cuts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kdenlive began in 2002 as a project started by Jason Wood, stalled, and was revived in 2005 by Jean-Baptiste Mardelle, who led its migration to the MLT framework and KDE 4. It became an official KDE project in 2015 and received a major refactor in the 2019.04 KDE Applications release.
Today it is maintained entirely by volunteer contributors under KDE e.V., the nonprofit that supports the KDE community, with no company or commercial tier behind it.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The interface is built in C++ with Qt, while all video and audio processing runs through the MLT Framework and FFmpeg underneath, giving Kdenlive support for virtually every common video and audio format alongside effects libraries like Frei0r and Movit.

Editors get an unlimited multi-track timeline, a title editor, proxy-clip workflows for smooth high-resolution editing, keyframeable effects, color scopes for grading, speech-to-text transcription, motion tracking and scene detection.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Kdenlive is entirely free, with no subscription, watermark, or feature-gated tier of any kind, distributed under the GPL for Windows, macOS and Linux.
Development is funded through voluntary donations to KDE e.V. rather than product revenue, which keeps the editor free while supporting ongoing contributor work and infrastructure.

features.title: Unlimited multi-track timeline
features.description: Supports an unlimited number of video and audio tracks for complex edits.

features.title: Built-in title editor
features.description: Create and animate text and title clips directly inside the application.

features.title: Proxy clip editing
features.description: Edit smoothly with high-resolution footage by working from lower-resolution proxies.

features.title: Keyframeable effects and transitions
features.description: Apply and animate effects and transitions with fine-grained keyframe control.

features.title: Color scopes
features.description: Waveform, histogram and vectorscope tools for color correction and grading.

features.title: Speech-to-text transcription
features.description: Automatically transcribe spoken audio in a project's clips.

features.title: Motion tracking and scene detection
features.description: Track motion within footage and automatically detect scene cuts.

features.title: Broad format support
features.description: Handles nearly any video or audio format via the underlying FFmpeg and MLT stack.

pros: Completely free with no watermarks or feature paywalls
Professional-grade feature set rivaling some paid editors
Cross-platform support for Windows, macOS and Linux
Active, KDE-backed volunteer development community
Strong integration with the Linux desktop ecosystem
cons: Can be less stable than mature commercial editors on complex projects
Steeper learning curve than simplified editors like OpenShot
Color grading and audio post-production tools are less advanced than DaVinci Resolve's Fairlight and color pages
Rendering performance depends heavily on system hardware and the underlying FFmpeg build
No official paid support channel

faqs.question: Is Kdenlive really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kdenlive is entirely free and open source under the GPL, with no paid tiers, watermarks, or subscriptions.

faqs.question: What platforms does Kdenlive support?
faqs.answer: Kdenlive runs on Windows, macOS and Linux.

faqs.question: What engine powers Kdenlive?
faqs.answer: Kdenlive's interface is built with Qt and KDE Frameworks, while video and audio processing runs through the MLT Framework and FFmpeg.

faqs.question: Who develops Kdenlive?
faqs.answer: Kdenlive is developed by volunteer contributors under KDE e.V., the nonprofit organization behind the KDE community.

faqs.question: How does Kdenlive compare to DaVinci Resolve?
faqs.answer: Kdenlive is fully free and open source and lighter weight, while DaVinci Resolve offers stronger color grading and audio tools with an optional paid Studio tier.

faqs.question: Can I use Kdenlive for commercial projects?
faqs.answer: Yes, the GPL license permits using Kdenlive to produce and sell commercial video work.

use_cases.title: Independent content creation
use_cases.description: YouTubers and independent creators edit videos without paying for a subscription-based editor.

use_cases.title: Linux desktop video editing
use_cases.description: Linux users get a fully native, actively maintained non-linear editor integrated with their desktop.

use_cases.title: Educational and student film editing
use_cases.description: Students and educators use Kdenlive to teach and practice video editing without licensing costs.

features: All effects and export formats included

enrichment.short_description: Keap is an all-in-one CRM and marketing automation platform for small businesses, formerly known as Infusionsoft.
enrichment.seo_title: Keap Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Keap, formerly Infusionsoft, review covering CRM, email and SMS automation and invoicing pricing from about 249 dollars per month, plus alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Keap is a customer relationship management and sales-and-marketing automation platform built specifically for small businesses, solopreneurs, and service-based companies. It was founded in 2001 in Chandler, Arizona by brothers Clate Mask, who became CEO, and Scott Martineau, initially under the name eNovaSys; the company rebranded to Infusionsoft in 2004 and built its reputation over the following fifteen years as one of the most powerful, and notoriously complex, marketing automation engines available to small businesses, combining CRM contact records with visual automation-sequence building, email marketing, and e-commerce tools. In 2019 the company rebranded again from Infusionsoft to Keap, a change intended to signal a simplified, more approachable product line aimed at non-technical small-business owners who had found Infusionsoft's automation builder powerful but difficult to learn. In 2024, Keap was acquired by Thryv, a publicly traded small-business software company that itself offers a competing all-in-one business management platform, and Keap has continued to operate as a distinct product line and brand under Thryv's ownership. Functionally, Keap centers on a contact-record CRM that tracks every interaction, deal, and pipeline stage for a customer, layered with marketing automation tools that let a business build multi-step email and SMS sequences, tag and segment contacts, and trigger follow-ups automatically based on customer behavior; around that core, Keap adds a visual sales pipeline for managing deals, landing page and web form builders for lead capture, appointment scheduling, quote and invoice and payment collection tools, and a mobile app for managing contacts, calls, and texts on the go. As of 2026, Keap has moved away from its older feature-gated, multi-tier plan structure toward a smaller number of higher-priced bundles that include the full platform: reporting on current plans generally shows a Pro tier around $249 per month, a Max tier around $299 per month with more advanced automation and lead-scoring capability, and an Ultimate tier around $349 per month, with each tier's price scaling upward based on how many contacts a business needs to store, starting around 1,500 to 2,500 contacts, and how many user seats are needed. Plans typically include two user licenses, with additional users costing roughly $39 to $59 per user per month, and most new customers on the Pro and Max tiers are required to purchase a one-time Kickstart onboarding and coaching package, reported at roughly $499 to $999, to help set up automations and migrate data. Keap also sells optional add-on capacity for SMS and voice minutes in tiered bundles, for example roughly $24 per month for 1,000 extra texts and 300 voice minutes, scaling up to around $279 per month for 25,000 texts and 2,000 minutes, plus per-message overage charges once bundled allowances are exceeded. Keap's target customer is the solo entrepreneur or small service business, such as coaches, consultants, agencies, and contractors, with a few hundred to a few thousand contacts that wants a single system combining CRM, email and SMS marketing, and basic sales-pipeline and invoicing tools without stitching together multiple point products. In the CRM and marketing-automation market, Keap is most often compared against HubSpot, which offers a more generous free and low-cost entry tier but can become expensive at scale, ActiveCampaign, seen as a more affordable, marketing-automation-first alternative, and entry-level Salesforce products, as well as broader small-business platforms like Thryv's own core product. Reviewers consistently note that Keap's key differentiators are its deep, purpose-built small-business automation engine, a legacy of its Infusionsoft roots, and integrated payments and invoicing, while its most commonly cited drawbacks are its relatively high starting price for a small-business tool, the mandatory onboarding fee, and a steeper learning curve than lighter competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Keap was founded in 2001 in Chandler, Arizona by Clate Mask and Scott Martineau as eNovaSys, rebranded to Infusionsoft in 2004, and rebranded again to Keap in 2019 to simplify its product for non-technical small business owners. Thryv acquired Keap in 2024.
The platform combines a contact-record CRM, marketing automation, sales pipeline management, and invoicing into one system aimed at small and service-based businesses.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Keap's core is a CRM paired with a visual automation builder for multi-step email and SMS sequences triggered by customer behavior, alongside a sales pipeline for tracking deals.

Additional tools include landing page and form builders, appointment scheduling, quote and invoice generation with payment collection, and a mobile app for managing contacts and communication on the go.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Keap's current plans are Pro at roughly $249 per month, Max at roughly $299 per month, and Ultimate at roughly $349 per month, billed annually, each including two user licenses with contact limits that scale by tier.

Additional users cost roughly $39 to $59 per month each, and new Pro and Max customers are generally required to purchase a one-time Kickstart onboarding package priced at roughly $499 to $999, with optional SMS and voice minute bundles available on top.

features.title: Contact CRM and sales pipeline
features.description: Tracks every customer interaction, deal stage and pipeline status in one system.

features.title: Email and SMS automation
features.description: Visual automation builder for multi-step, behavior-triggered marketing sequences.

features.title: Landing pages and web forms
features.description: Built-in tools for capturing leads directly into the CRM.

features.title: Appointment scheduling
features.description: Lets prospects and customers book time directly with the business.

features.title: Quotes, invoicing and payments
features.description: Generate quotes and invoices and collect payments without a separate tool.

features.title: Mobile app

faqs.question: What was Keap previously called?
faqs.answer: Keap was previously known as Infusionsoft before rebranding in 2019, and before that as eNovaSys.

faqs.question: Who founded Keap?
faqs.answer: Keap was founded in 2001 by Clate Mask and Scott Martineau in Chandler, Arizona.

faqs.question: Who owns Keap now?
faqs.answer: Keap has been owned by Thryv, a small-business software company, since 2024.

faqs.question: How much does Keap cost?
faqs.answer: Keap's plans run roughly from $249 per month for Pro to $349 per month for Ultimate, billed annually, plus additional user and onboarding fees.

faqs.question: Does Keap charge for onboarding?
faqs.answer: Yes, most new Pro and Max customers must purchase a one-time Kickstart onboarding package priced at roughly $499 to $999.

faqs.question: Who are Keap's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Keap's main competitors include HubSpot, ActiveCampaign and entry-level Salesforce products.

enrichment.short_description: Keeper Security is a zero-knowledge password manager and cybersecurity platform offering password, secrets and privileged access management.
enrichment.seo_title: Keeper Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Keeper Security review: encrypted password manager, BreachWatch monitoring and enterprise PAM pricing from about 4 dollars per month, plus alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Keeper Security, commonly just Keeper and distinct from the unrelated calendar tool Keeper.sh, is a cybersecurity company founded in 2009 by Darren Guccione and Craig Lurey, who remain the company's owners; Keeper is headquartered in Chicago, Illinois, with additional offices in California, Ireland, and the Philippines, and reported employee counts in 2026 generally fall in the 700-plus range, with revenue reported around $73.5 million. The company has raised roughly $60 million in funding, most notably a Series D round from Insight Partners in May 2023, and has built its business around a zero-knowledge security architecture, meaning Keeper's own servers never have access to a customer's unencrypted vault data or master password: all encryption and decryption happens locally on the user's device using AES-256 encryption combined with elliptic curve cryptography, and Keeper has begun rolling out quantum-resistant cryptography, announced for Q1 2026, to future-proof its encryption against advances in quantum computing. At its core, Keeper is a password manager: it stores usernames, passwords, payment cards, identity documents, and secure notes in an encrypted vault synced across unlimited devices, with browser extensions, desktop apps, and mobile apps, biometric unlock via fingerprint or Face ID, a built-in password generator, and secure record and folder sharing between users. Around that core, Keeper has expanded into a broader cybersecurity platform: BreachWatch continuously monitors the dark web for credentials tied to a user's stored accounts and alerts them to breaches; KeeperChat provides end-to-end encrypted messaging; Keeper Secrets Manager is a cloud-native secrets management product for developers and DevOps teams to secure API keys, database credentials, and certificates without hardcoding them; Keeper Connection Manager provides browser-based, agentless remote access to infrastructure; and KeeperPAM bundles these enterprise capabilities into a privileged access management suite aimed at replacing legacy PAM tools. Keeper holds a notably deep set of compliance certifications for a company of its size, including FedRAMP High authorization, FIPS 140-3 validation, and ITAR compliance, which has made it a common choice among United States government, defense, and highly regulated enterprise customers. On pricing, Keeper follows a tiered subscription model billed primarily annually: a Personal plan with unlimited password, identity, and payment storage, unlimited devices and sync, secure sharing, emergency access, and 24/7 support is priced at roughly $4 per month when billed annually, up from an earlier roughly $2.92 per month rate; a Family plan supporting up to five private vaults plus 10GB of shared secure file storage runs a modest premium over the Personal plan; and optional add-ons include BreachWatch dark web monitoring and additional secure file storage. On the business side, Keeper offers a Business Starter plan around $2 per user per month, a Business plan around $3.75 per user per month, an Enterprise plan generally listed around $6 to $7.50 per user per month, and Keeper Enterprise Plus, which layers in the advanced PAM and secrets-management capabilities, typically priced from roughly $12 to $15-plus per user per month, all generally billed annually with per-seat business pricing. Keeper's target audience spans individual consumers and families wanting straightforward password protection, up through IT and security teams at mid-market and large enterprises, government agencies, and regulated industries such as finance, healthcare, and defense that need centralized credential governance, compliance reporting, and privileged access controls alongside basic password management. In the password manager and identity-security market, Keeper competes most directly with 1Password, which leads on device-trust and Extended Access Management features and includes generous guest-account allowances on business plans, Bitwarden, the only fully open-source option among the major players with self-hosting available and the lowest listed enterprise per-seat pricing, and Dashlane, differentiated by AI-driven credential risk scoring and Confidential SSO built on AWS Nitro Enclaves, as well as legacy competitor LastPass. Reviewers generally position Keeper's key differentiators as its unusually deep compliance certifications and its combination of consumer password management with enterprise-grade secrets management and privileged access management in a single vendor, while its relative weaknesses cited by some reviewers include a less polished interface than 1Password or Dashlane and pricing that has trended upward across recent renewal cycles.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Keeper Security was founded in 2009 by Darren Guccione and Craig Lurey and is headquartered in Chicago, with offices in California, Ireland and the Philippines. The company has raised roughly $60 million, including a Series D round from Insight Partners in 2023.
Keeper uses a zero-knowledge architecture where encryption and decryption happen locally on the user's device, so Keeper's own servers never see unencrypted vault data or master passwords.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Beyond core password storage, Keeper includes BreachWatch dark web monitoring, KeeperChat encrypted messaging, Keeper Secrets Manager for DevOps credential storage, Keeper Connection Manager for agentless remote access, and KeeperPAM for privileged access management.

The company holds deep compliance certifications including FedRAMP High, FIPS 140-3 and ITAR, making it a common choice for government, defense and regulated enterprise customers, and it has begun rolling out quantum-resistant cryptography.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Consumer pricing starts with a Personal plan at roughly $4 per month billed annually, and a Family plan covering up to five vaults with 10GB of shared storage at a modest premium.

Business pricing runs from roughly $2 per user per month for Business Starter up to $12 to $15-plus per user per month for Enterprise Plus, which adds KeeperPAM and secrets management on top of core password management.

Family and business plans scale from individuals to large enterprises
cons: Pricing has trended upward across recent renewal cycles
Interface seen as less polished than 1Password or Dashlane by some reviewers
Full enterprise feature set with KeeperPAM is priced significantly higher than basic tiers
Not open source, unlike Bitwarden
Some advanced features require separate add-on purchases

faqs.answer: No. Keeper Security (keepersecurity.com) is a password manager, while Keeper.sh is an unrelated open-source calendar sync tool.

faqs.answer: The Personal plan costs roughly $4 per month when billed annually.

faqs.answer: No, Keeper is closed source, unlike its competitor Bitwarden.

faqs.answer: Keeper holds FedRAMP High authorization, FIPS 140-3 validation, and ITAR compliance.

faqs.answer: Keeper Security was founded in 2009 by Darren Guccione and Craig Lurey.

enrichment.short_description: Keeper.sh is an open-source calendar sync tool that aggregates availability across Google, Outlook, iCloud and CalDAV calendars.
enrichment.seo_title: Keeper.sh Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Keeper.sh review: open-source calendar sync with privacy-first busy and free sharing, self-hosting and an AI-ready MCP server, pricing from free to 5 dollars.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Keeper.sh is a distinct, unrelated product from the password manager Keeper Security (keepersecurity.com); despite the similar name, Keeper.sh is an open-source calendar synchronization tool built and maintained by developer Rida F'kih and released under the AGPL-3.0 license, with its source code hosted publicly on GitHub. The product solves a specific, common scheduling problem: professionals who juggle multiple calendars across different accounts or organizations, for example a personal Google account, a work Outlook account, and a freelance or school calendar, frequently experience double-bookings because none of those calendars can see events from the others. Keeper.sh addresses this with a pull-compare-push sync engine that continuously reads events from every connected calendar, compares them, and pushes corresponding placeholder events into the other connected calendars so that busy and free time is consistently reflected everywhere, while using conflict-reconciliation logic and race-condition prevention to keep everything in sync as source events are added, moved, or deleted. By default, Keeper.sh only shares busy and free time blocks between calendars and strips out event titles, descriptions, locations, and attendee lists before syncing, prioritizing privacy; users can override this on a per-calendar basis to include titles, descriptions, custom templates, or filtered event types if they want more detail to carry over. Supported calendar connections include OAuth-based integration with Google Calendar and Microsoft Outlook, both of which use incremental sync that fetches only changed events for efficiency, CalDAV-based integration with FastMail, iCloud, and generic CalDAV servers, and read-only ingestion of standard iCal and ICS feed URLs; Keeper.sh also generates its own unique, token-authenticated iCal feed for each user so external calendar apps can subscribe to a unified view. Beyond simple two-way syncing, the tool supports flexible source-to-destination mapping, where one source calendar can push to many destinations, or many sources can consolidate into one, tags synced events with a distinguishing keeper.sh suffix or a category label in Outlook so users can tell which entries are synced placeholders versus original events, and provides real-time sync status updates over WebSocket. A notable modern addition is an optional Model Context Protocol server, which exposes read-only calendar access to AI agents and assistants, including Claude Code and Claude Desktop, through OAuth 2.1 authentication and three defined tools for listing calendars, retrieving events by date range, and counting synced events, making Keeper.sh one of a small number of calendar tools designed to be directly queryable by AI agents. Under the hood, Keeper.sh is built on a modern self-hostable stack of PostgreSQL, Redis, Bun, and a Vite and React frontend, and the project explicitly supports self-hosting through a standalone Docker Compose file, services-only containers, or fully separated microservices, in addition to a hosted cloud version. Pricing follows a simple freemium structure: a Free tier supports 2 connected accounts with 2 calendars each, syncing roughly every 30 minutes, and a basic iCal feed; a Pro tier at roughly $5 per month removes those limits, offering unlimited accounts and calendars, syncing as frequently as every minute, and priority support; and the self-hosted deployment option includes all Pro-tier features at no cost beyond the user's own infrastructure. Keeper.sh's target audience is professionals and small teams managing three or more calendars across separate organizations or ecosystems, including freelancers, consultants, people balancing a day job with side projects, and anyone who needs collision-free scheduling without manually copying events by hand. In the calendar-sync category, it competes with tools such as OneCal, CalendarBridge, and Reclaim.ai, a broader productivity suite that syncs calendars as one feature among many, though it supports only Google Calendar; Keeper.sh differentiates itself primarily through being fully open source and self-hostable, its privacy-first default of syncing only free and busy status, and its built-in MCP server for AI-agent calendar access, none of which the closed-source commercial alternatives offer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Keeper.sh is an open-source calendar synchronization tool, unrelated to the password manager Keeper Security, built by developer Rida F'kih and released under the AGPL-3.0 license. It solves double-booking problems for people juggling multiple calendars across accounts and organizations.
The tool uses a pull-compare-push sync engine to keep busy and free time consistent across every connected calendar, with conflict reconciliation and race-condition prevention built in.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Keeper.sh connects to Google Calendar and Outlook via OAuth, to FastMail and iCloud via CalDAV, and to any generic iCal or ICS feed in read-only mode, with flexible one-to-many or many-to-one mapping between calendars.

By default only busy and free time is shared, with event titles, descriptions and attendees stripped out unless a user opts in per calendar. An optional MCP server also gives AI agents read-only access to calendar data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: A Free tier covers 2 accounts with 2 calendars each and syncs every 30 minutes, while the Pro tier at roughly $5 per month removes those limits and syncs every minute with priority support.
Keeper.sh can also be self-hosted via Docker, which includes all Pro-tier features at no cost beyond the user's own infrastructure.

Built-in MCP server enables AI agents to query calendars directly
Affordable Pro tier with fast 1-minute sync intervals
Supports a wide range of calendar providers and protocols
cons: Free tier is limited to 2 accounts and 2 calendars each
Built and maintained by a single developer, raising long-term support questions
Newer product with a smaller track record than established competitors
Self-hosting requires technical setup involving PostgreSQL, Redis and Docker
Free tier sync interval of 30 minutes is slow compared to the paid tier

faqs.question: Is Keeper.sh the same as Keeper Security?
faqs.answer: No, Keeper.sh is an entirely different, unrelated product from the Keeper Security password manager.

faqs.question: Is Keeper.sh free?
faqs.answer: Yes, a free tier exists with 2 accounts and 2 calendars each; the Pro tier costs roughly $5 per month.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Keeper.sh?
faqs.answer: Yes, Keeper.sh can be self-hosted via Docker, including all Pro-tier features.

faqs.question: What calendars does Keeper.sh support?
faqs.answer: Keeper.sh supports Google Calendar, Outlook, iCloud, FastMail, generic CalDAV servers and ICS feeds.

faqs.question: Does Keeper.sh share event details?
faqs.answer: No, by default it only shares busy and free time; sharing titles or descriptions is opt-in per calendar.

faqs.question: Does Keeper.sh work with AI assistants?
faqs.answer: Yes, an optional MCP server gives AI agents like Claude read-only access to calendar data.

enrichment.short_description: KeeWeb is a free, open-source, cross-platform password manager fully compatible with KeePass .kdbx database files.
enrichment.seo_title: KeeWeb Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: KeeWeb review: a free open-source password manager compatible with KeePass, with cloud storage sync, self-hosting and no subscription fees.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: KeeWeb is a free and open-source password manager, released under the permissive MIT license, that was originally created in 2015 by Antelle, also known as Dimitri Witkowski, a full-stack software developer based in the Netherlands who works full time at Booking.com and built KeeWeb as an open-source side project. KeeWeb's defining characteristic is full compatibility with the KeePass ecosystem: it reads and writes the same .kdbx database file format used by KeePass and KeePassXC, meaning users already invested in KeePass-format vaults can open, edit, and save their existing password databases in KeeWeb without any conversion, and vice versa, giving them freedom to move between clients without vendor lock-in. Those .kdbx files are protected using industry-standard encryption, typically AES-256 or ChaCha20-Poly1305, and KeeWeb performs all encryption and decryption locally, never transmitting the master password or decrypted vault contents to a server it controls, since KeeWeb itself has no backend infrastructure of its own. KeeWeb is genuinely cross-platform: it ships as native-feeling desktop applications for Windows, macOS, and Linux built with Electron, and it also runs entirely as a web application in any modern browser, distributed as effectively a single self-contained HTML file, optionally paired with a service worker for offline access, which means it can be trivially self-hosted on a personal web server, a static file host, or a home lab box with zero server-side logic required. For storing and syncing the encrypted database file itself, KeeWeb integrates directly with popular cloud storage providers including Dropbox, Google Drive, OneDrive, and WebDAV, as well as supporting purely local file storage, so users can choose their own sync mechanism rather than being tied to a proprietary cloud. Functionally, KeeWeb supports opening and searching across multiple database files simultaneously, presenting results from all open files in one unified list; it includes a built-in password generator, auto-type functionality for filling credentials into other applications, support for one-time password fields stored alongside credentials, file attachments within entries, and customizable interface themes including dark mode. As development has continued, the project has been sustained on a community basis rather than by a company or full-time maintainer; its original creator stepped back from active day-to-day development in the early 2020s and the project has continued as what its own repository describes as a hobby project without a dedicated organization, funded informally through GitHub Sponsors and Open Collective donations, though the codebase has continued to receive updates and releases into 2026. Because KeeWeb is entirely free, open source, and self-hostable, it has no pricing tiers, subscription plans, or premium features of any kind, the entire application is available at no cost, with its only real cost being that a user must supply their own file storage or cloud account for syncing. KeeWeb's target audience is privacy-conscious individuals and technically inclined users who are already using or want to use the open KeePass file format, particularly those who want a more modern interface than the traditional KeePass or KeePassXC desktop clients, or who want browser-based or self-hosted access to their vault without paying for a commercial password manager's cloud subscription. In the password manager category, KeeWeb's closest peers are other KeePass-format-compatible clients such as KeePassXC and the original KeePass, with which it is directly interoperable rather than competitive, and it is more broadly an alternative to commercial cloud password managers such as Keeper Security, 1Password, Bitwarden, and Dashlane; KeeWeb differentiates itself from those commercial products by having no company, no subscription, no seat pricing, and no proprietary vault format, instead giving users full ownership of a portable, standards-based encrypted file that can be opened by multiple independent applications, at the cost of the polished onboarding, customer support, and integrated breach-monitoring features that funded commercial products provide.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: KeeWeb was created in 2015 by Antelle, a Netherlands-based developer, as an open-source password manager fully compatible with the KeePass .kdbx file format. It is released under the MIT license and has continued as a community-maintained hobby project.
It ships as desktop apps for Windows, macOS and Linux, and as a browser-based web app distributed as a single self-contained HTML file, making it easy to self-host anywhere.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: KeeWeb reads and writes standard .kdbx database files encrypted with AES-256 or ChaCha20-Poly1305, with all encryption and decryption happening locally on the user's device.

It integrates with Dropbox, Google Drive, OneDrive and WebDAV for syncing, supports opening and searching across multiple database files at once, and includes a password generator, auto-type, and customizable themes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: KeeWeb is entirely free and open source with no subscription tiers, premium features, or paid add-ons of any kind.
The only ongoing cost is whatever cloud storage or hosting a user chooses to sync or host their .kdbx database file, since KeeWeb itself has no backend service to pay for.

Full interoperability with KeePass and KeePassXC via the .kdbx format
No vendor lock-in, works with any cloud storage or local files
Can be self-hosted as a single static HTML file
Available as both a desktop app and a browser-based web app
cons: No dedicated official mobile app
Maintained as a community hobby project without a full-time maintainer
No built-in breach monitoring or dark web alerts
Support is community-based with no formal service level agreement
Feature development has slowed compared to commercial competitors

faqs.question: Is KeeWeb free?
faqs.answer: Yes, KeeWeb is entirely free and open source under the MIT license.

faqs.question: Is KeeWeb compatible with KeePass?
faqs.answer: Yes, KeeWeb fully supports the .kdbx database format used by KeePass and KeePassXC.

faqs.question: Who created KeeWeb?
faqs.answer: KeeWeb was created in 2015 by Antelle, also known as Dimitri Witkowski, a developer based in the Netherlands.

faqs.question: Can KeeWeb sync with cloud storage?
faqs.answer: Yes, KeeWeb integrates with Dropbox, Google Drive, OneDrive and WebDAV.

faqs.question: Is KeeWeb self-hostable?
faqs.answer: Yes, KeeWeb can be self-hosted as a single self-contained HTML file on any web server.

faqs.question: Does KeeWeb have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: There is no official KeeWeb mobile app; users typically use another KeePass-compatible mobile app with the same .kdbx file.

enrichment.short_description: Keila is an open-source, privacy-focused email newsletter platform that can be self-hosted or used as an EU-hosted managed cloud service.
enrichment.seo_title: Keila Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Keila review: open-source Mailchimp alternative with self-hosting, EU-hosted cloud pricing from 8 euros per month, and GDPR-friendly analytics.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Keila is an open-source email newsletter and marketing platform built with the Elixir programming language and the Phoenix web framework, developed by the GitHub organization and maintainer pentacent and operated commercially as Pentacent, Philipp Schmieder Medien, a Germany-based operation; the software itself is released under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3, a strong copyleft license, and its source code is publicly hosted on GitHub where the project has accumulated over 2,000 stars. Keila positions itself explicitly as an open-source alternative to commercial newsletter and email marketing tools such as Mailchimp and Sendinblue (Brevo), built around a stated philosophy of privacy-first, GDPR-friendly email marketing with no proprietary premium features gated behind the open-source version, the company states plainly that Keila is 100 percent open source with no proprietary components, meaning the self-hosted version is functionally identical to the paid, managed version. The product covers the core workflow of running an email newsletter: composing campaigns using a visual block-based editor, plain Markdown, or full custom HTML and MJML templates for teams wanting complete design control; building and embedding customizable sign-up forms with configurable fields, checkboxes, and dropdowns to grow a subscriber list; segmenting contacts using either a visual segment editor or a more powerful query-style segment language to target campaigns at specific subsets of subscribers; and reviewing privacy-conscious campaign analytics that can be disabled entirely with one click for organizations that do not want to track opens and clicks at all. Keila includes bot protection for signup forms via hCaptcha or Friendly Captcha, double opt-in confirmation flows, Liquid templating syntax for personalizing email content with dynamic contact or JSON data, and a REST API for programmatically managing contacts and campaigns, with a Zapier integration available in beta. For actually sending mail, Keila does not operate as a closed platform locked to its own infrastructure, it supports direct SMTP configuration as well as native integrations with major transactional email providers including Amazon SES, SendGrid, Mailgun, and Postmark, so self-hosters can route mail through whichever provider they already trust. Keila can be run in two ways: fully self-hosted via an official Docker image on a user's own server, which is entirely free of charge beyond the user's own infrastructure and mail-sending provider costs; or as Keila Cloud, a managed hosted version of the software run entirely on servers located in Germany and France, marketed specifically on the basis of strict European Union data-privacy standards. Keila Cloud pricing is structured in six volume-based tiers rather than by contact count, since contacts and projects are unlimited on every plan: an XS plan at EUR 8 per month for up to 2,000 emails sent, an S plan at EUR 16 per month for up to 5,000 emails, an M plan at EUR 32 per month for up to 15,000 emails, an L plan at EUR 64 per month for up to 50,000 emails, an XL plan at EUR 128 per month for up to 100,000 emails, and an XXL plan at EUR 256 per month for up to 250,000 emails; notably, Keila deliberately offers no permanent free tier, with the company explaining its reasoning directly on its pricing page, that a free email tool typically means the user's data is the real product, so instead Keila offers discretionary discounts for open-source projects and registered nonprofits evaluated case by case. Keila's target audience includes privacy-conscious small businesses, nonprofits, community organizations, political and advocacy groups, open-source projects, and developers who want either a lightweight self-hosted newsletter tool with no vendor lock-in or a paid European-hosted alternative to Mailchimp-style platforms for GDPR-compliance reasons. Within the open-source newsletter category, Keila is most often compared with Listmonk, a Go-based, single-binary newsletter manager that is generally regarded as lighter-weight and able to run on cheaper infrastructure at large subscriber volumes, Mailcoach, a Laravel-based tool from Spatie that supports bring-your-own-provider sending and offers more built-in automation workflows than Keila, and Mautic, a much larger, more full-featured open-source marketing-automation platform used by over 200,000 organizations; reviewers generally describe Keila as the newest and most minimally designed of these tools, with a cleaner and more modern interface but a smaller community and fewer production-hardened integrations than its more established competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Keila is an open-source email newsletter platform built in Elixir and Phoenix, developed by pentacent and operated by the Germany-based Pentacent, Philipp Schmieder Medien. It is released under the AGPLv3 license with no proprietary features gated behind the paid version.
It can be run fully self-hosted via Docker at no cost, or used as Keila Cloud, a managed service hosted entirely in Germany and France for strict European privacy standards.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Keila offers a visual block editor plus Markdown and MJML support for composing campaigns, customizable sign-up forms, and contact segmentation through both a visual editor and a query-style segment language.

It includes privacy-respecting analytics that can be disabled with one click, bot protection via hCaptcha or Friendly Captcha, double opt-in, Liquid templating for personalization, and a REST API with a beta Zapier integration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Self-hosting Keila via Docker is completely free, with unlimited contacts and projects and no feature restrictions compared to the paid cloud version.
Keila Cloud is priced in six volume-based tiers from EUR 8 per month for 2,000 emails up to EUR 256 per month for 250,000 emails, with unlimited contacts on every tier and no permanent free plan.

features.title: Visual and Markdown email editor
features.description: Compose campaigns with a block-based visual editor, Markdown, or custom MJML templates.

features.title: Sign-up form builder
features.description: Create and embed customizable subscription forms with configurable fields.

features.title: Contact segmentation
features.description: Segment contacts using a visual editor or a powerful query-style segment language.

features.title: Privacy-respecting analytics
features.description: Campaign analytics that can be fully disabled with one click for privacy-first organizations.

features.title: Double opt-in and bot protection
features.description: hCaptcha or Friendly Captcha integration plus double opt-in confirmation flows.

features.title: Liquid personalization
features.description: Dynamic email content personalization using the Liquid templating language.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Programmatic management of contacts and campaigns, with a beta Zapier integration.

features.title: Bring-your-own email provider
features.description: Send via SMTP, Amazon SES, SendGrid, Mailgun or Postmark.

pros: 100 percent open source with no proprietary premium features
Self-hosted version is free and functionally identical to paid Keila Cloud
EU-hosted managed option in Germany and France supports strict GDPR compliance
Privacy-first analytics that can be fully disabled
Bring-your-own email provider flexibility
cons: No permanent free tier for the managed Keila Cloud service
Smaller community and fewer integrations than Mailchimp or Mautic
Fewer built-in automation workflows than competitors like Mailcoach
Newer and less production-hardened than more established open-source tools like Listmonk
Self-hosting requires familiarity with Elixir and Docker

faqs.question: Is Keila free?
faqs.answer: The self-hosted version is completely free; the managed Keila Cloud service starts at EUR 8 per month.

faqs.question: Is Keila open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, Keila is 100 percent open source under the AGPLv3 license with no gated premium features.

faqs.question: Where is Keila Cloud hosted?
faqs.answer: Keila Cloud runs entirely on servers located in Germany and France.

faqs.question: What email providers does Keila support?
faqs.answer: Keila supports direct SMTP as well as Amazon SES, SendGrid, Mailgun and Postmark.

faqs.question: Does Keila offer automation workflows?
faqs.answer: Keila offers basic automation but fewer built-in workflows than competitors like Mailcoach or Mautic.

faqs.question: How does Keila compare to Mailchimp?
faqs.answer: Keila is an open-source, self-hostable alternative to Mailchimp with unlimited contacts on every plan and an EU-hosted option for GDPR compliance.

use_cases.title: GDPR-conscious European newsletters
use_cases.description: EU businesses and nonprofits send newsletters through EU-hosted infrastructure to meet strict privacy requirements.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted developer newsletter
use_cases.description: Developers and open-source projects self-host Keila for full control with no per-contact fees.

use_cases.title: Nonprofit and advocacy campaigns
use_cases.description: Nonprofits and community groups run email campaigns while respecting subscriber privacy.

features: All features included, no premium gate
